network.overall_strategy: This network centers glycine synthesis on literature-supported hub molecules HCN, formaldehyde, ammonia, aminoacetonitrile, and glyoxylate. Hydrothermal chemistry supplies reduced carbon feedstocks and glyoxylate via CO2 fixation on iron-sulfur and related minerals under alkaline serpentinizing conditions. Surface environments generate and concentrate HCN chemistry, formaldehyde-derived Strecker intermediates, cyanosulfidic aminoacetonitrile, and HCN polymer reservoirs under UV, wet-dry, and eutectic conditions. Biochemical/prebiotic settings complete slow hydrolyses and assembly steps, especially conversion of aminoacetonitrile through glycinamide or hydantoin/N-carbamoylglycine to glycine, while also allowing peptide formation from glycine. The ten target-reaching pathways are distinct variants grounded in the supplied literature: canonical Strecker through glycinamide, Bucherer-Bergs detour, glycolonitrile-first Strecker branch, cyanosulfidic aminoacetonitrile route, FTT-fed Strecker, spark-atmosphere-fed Strecker, NH4CN frozen polymer hydrolysis, HCN polymer hydrolysis in warm aqueous media, ferroan brucite glyoxylate+nitrate synthesis, and UV/mineral-assisted glyoxylate amination.

reactions.name: Hydrothermal CO2 reduction to formate

conditions.other: alkaline vent conditions, pH 9-11, H2-rich serpentinizing system

reactions.mechanism: Mineral-surface CO2 hydrogenation generates formate as a primary C1 fixation product under alkaline hydrothermal conditions.
reactions.reasoning: Preiner et al. (2020) showed greigite and magnetite catalyze CO2 + H2 reduction to formate at 100C in alkaline hydrothermal analog systems; this is a core hydrothermal feedstock step for later surface chemistry.

reactions.name: Hydrothermal CO2 fixation to pyruvate

conditions.other: alkaline vent chemistry; pyruvate detected as low-yield product

reactions.mechanism: Multielectron mineral-catalyzed CO2 fixation under vent conditions furnishes C3 keto-acid products including pyruvate.
reactions.reasoning: Preiner et al. (2020) reported pyruvate formation from CO2 and H2 on hydrothermal minerals including magnetite and greigite at 100C; Beyazay et al. (2023) further supported low-temperature mineral-catalyzed pyruvate production in serpentinizing systems.

reactions.name: Hydrothermal glyoxylate generation in CO2-reducing mineral systems

conditions.catalyst: Fe0 and FeS as hydrothermal reducing/mineral surfaces
conditions.other: serpentinizing alkaline setting; glyoxylate included as available C2 carbonyl feedstock for ferroan-brucite glycine synthesis

reactions.mechanism: Hydrothermal CO2-reducing networks provide small oxygenated carbonyl compounds including glyoxylate-like C2 substrates that can be captured in associated mineral compartments.
reactions.reasoning: The supplied literature identifies glyoxylate as the direct substrate in ferroan brucite-mediated glycine synthesis and places CO2-to-C1/C2/C3 carbon fixation in alkaline hydrothermal systems (Preiner et al., 2020; Wimmer et al., 2021). This edge is a conservative network-availability bridge rather than a claim of a single selective isolated reaction; it is included to connect hydrothermal carbon fixation to the experimentally demonstrated ferroan brucite glyoxylate route.

reactions.name: FTT/spark precursor generation of formaldehyde from CO-rich reduced gases

conditions.temperature: 360-600C generation zone with cooler condensation zone
conditions.pressure: 1 atm to local volcanic discharge conditions
conditions.catalyst: meteoritic/native iron with photocatalytic mineral-assisted surface processing
conditions.other: CO-rich atmosphere or volcanic lightning setting; formaldehyde subsequently dissolves into surface waters

reactions.mechanism: Reduced gas processing on metallic surfaces generates aldehydes, which are trapped in cooler aqueous surface reservoirs.
reactions.reasoning: Schlesinger and Miller (1983) showed CO-containing atmospheres can yield bioorganics comparable to reducing atmospheres, while Yoshino et al. (1971) described FTT-type amino-acid-forming systems via precursor generation on metallic catalysts. This reaction represents the literature-supported formaldehyde precursor pool entering surface chemistry.

reactions.name: Atmospheric/surface generation of HCN

conditions.temperature: 300-370K after quench from energetic atmospheric production
conditions.catalyst: TiO2 and sulfide mineral surfaces as receiving/photochemical interfaces
conditions.other: spark, UV, or volcanic lightning in CO-containing or transiently reducing atmospheres

reactions.mechanism: Energetic atmospheric chemistry generates HCN, which is deposited into surface environments where mineral surfaces aid concentration and photochemical persistence.
reactions.reasoning: Miller (1953, 1955, 1957) established atmospheric discharge production of HCN and amino-acid precursors; Schlesinger and Miller (1983) showed CO-rich atmospheres remain productive. This node is required by the literature because HCN is a master precursor for glycine synthesis.

reactions.name: Ammonia addition to formaldehyde

conditions.other: warm pond or evaporitic pool; near-neutral to mildly basic aqueous solution

reactions.mechanism: Nucleophilic addition of ammonia to formaldehyde gives aminomethanol, the first Strecker intermediate.
reactions.reasoning: Magrino, Pietrucci and Saitta (2021) mapped the aqueous Strecker free-energy landscape beginning with ammonia attack on formaldehyde to form aminomethanol. Mineral surfaces here are included for concentration in surface settings consistent with the prompt.

reactions.name: Dehydration of aminomethanol to methanimine

conditions.other: wet-dry microcycles or illuminated mineral interface

reactions.mechanism: Aminomethanol loses water to generate methanimine, the electrophilic imine used in Strecker cyanide addition.
reactions.reasoning: This is the second elementary step in the Strecker mechanism characterized by Magrino et al. (2021). The mineral assignment reflects prebiotic concentration/dehydration surfaces rather than a claim of exclusive catalytic necessity.

reactions.name: Cyanide addition to methanimine forming aminoacetonitrile

conditions.catalyst: surface concentration on clay and sulfide minerals
conditions.other: aqueous Strecker conditions; glycinonitrile formation barrier about 15 kcal/mol

reactions.mechanism: Cyanide attacks methanimine to furnish aminoacetonitrile, the central nitrile reservoir for glycine.
reactions.reasoning: Magrino et al. (2021) identified glycinonitrile formation from methanimine and cyanide as a key exergonic Strecker step with a ~15 kcal/mol barrier. Aminoacetonitrile is also highlighted in the supplied review as the major glycine hub.

reactions.name: Direct cyanohydrin formation to glycolonitrile

conditions.other: pH around 8; glycolonitrile strongly favored over aminoacetonitrile at low ammonia

reactions.mechanism: Formaldehyde reacts with cyanide to form the cyanohydrin glycolonitrile, a convergent nitrile reservoir that can later be aminated to aminoacetonitrile.
reactions.reasoning: Taillades, Commeyras et al. (1998) found that under realistic prebiotic conditions glycolonitrile forms overwhelmingly from HCN and formaldehyde, dominating product distribution and acting as a major branch point in glycine chemistry.

reactions.name: Ammonolysis of glycolonitrile to aminoacetonitrile

conditions.other: low-ammonia aqueous solution; branch from glycolonitrile toward Strecker nitrile pool

reactions.mechanism: Ammonia substitutes into the cyanohydrin-derived system to produce aminoacetonitrile under Strecker/Bucherer-relevant conditions.
reactions.reasoning: Taillades, Commeyras et al. (1998) explicitly discuss the low but crucial aminoacetonitrile fraction in equilibrium with glycolonitrile and its productive diversion by CO2. This reaction encodes that branch without inventing unsupported intermediates.

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of aminoacetonitrile to glycinamide

conditions.other: slow aqueous nitrile hydration; barrier about 23 kcal/mol

reactions.mechanism: Aminoacetonitrile undergoes nitrile hydration to the amide glycinamide, the penultimate canonical Strecker intermediate.
reactions.reasoning: Magrino et al. (2021) identified glycinamide as the intermediate in aqueous aminoacetonitrile hydrolysis, with nitrile hydration requiring roughly 23 kcal/mol.

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of glycinamide to glycine

conditions.other: slow aqueous amide hydrolysis; highest Strecker barrier about 31 kcal/mol

reactions.mechanism: Amide hydrolysis of glycinamide yields glycine, completing the canonical Strecker route.
reactions.reasoning: Magrino et al. (2021) showed final amide hydrolysis to glycine is the rate-limiting step of canonical Strecker completion with an approximately 31 kcal/mol barrier and strongly exergonic overall reaction.

reactions.name: Bucherer-Bergs capture of aminoacetonitrile by CO2 to hydantoin

conditions.catalyst: mineral concentration in bicarbonate-rich aqueous pool
conditions.other: pH about 8; CO2/bicarbonate productive trapping of aminoacetonitrile

reactions.mechanism: Aminoacetonitrile reacts with inorganic carbon to enter the hydantoin manifold, diverting nitrile hydrolysis through a carbamoyl-protected route.
reactions.reasoning: Taillades, Commeyras et al. (1998) showed CO2 catalyzes conversion of the small aminoacetonitrile fraction into hydantoin under realistic prebiotic conditions, productively shifting equilibrium toward glycine precursors. Pulletikurti et al. (2022) further demonstrated high hydantoin yields in bicarbonate buffer.

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of hydantoin to N-carbamoylglycine

conditions.other: prolonged aqueous hydrolysis in prebiotic pool

reactions.mechanism: Ring-opening hydrolysis of hydantoin gives N-carbamoylglycine as the protected amino-acid intermediate.
reactions.reasoning: The hydantoin-to-N-carbamoylglycine-to-glycine sequence is the canonical Bucherer-Bergs completion described by Taillades, Commeyras et al. (1998).

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of N-carbamoylglycine to glycine

conditions.other: decarbamoylating hydrolysis in aqueous prebiotic setting

reactions.mechanism: Final hydrolysis removes the carbamoyl group to liberate glycine.
reactions.reasoning: Taillades, Commeyras et al. (1998) identify N-carbamoylglycine as the hydrolysis precursor to glycine in the Bucherer-Bergs branch.

reactions.name: HCN oligomerization to DAMN-rich polymer reservoir

conditions.catalyst: mineral concentration under eutectic freezing or illuminated surfaces
conditions.other: requires concentrated HCN above about 0.01-0.1 M or eutectic freezing near -23.4C; polymerization via dimer/trimer/tetramer sequence

reactions.mechanism: Concentrated HCN oligomerizes through cyanide-driven steps to DAMN and higher polymers, creating a reservoir that later hydrolyzes to amino acids including glycine.
reactions.reasoning: Sanchez, Ferris and Orgel (1966, 1967) established HCN polymerization kinetics and threshold concentrations; the supplied review emphasizes DAMN and HCN polymer reservoirs as a distinct glycine source, especially under eutectic concentration.

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of HCN polymer reservoir to glycine

conditions.other: acidic or basic hydrolysis over days to weeks; glycine major amino-acid product

reactions.mechanism: Acid/base hydrolysis of HCN oligomer/polymer material releases amino acids, with glycine predominating across many conditions.
reactions.reasoning: Ruiz-Bermejo et al. (2013) found glycine to be the most abundant amino acid from HCN polymer hydrolysis under all tested conditions; Oró's early ammoniacal HCN polymer studies likewise yielded glycine prominently.

reactions.name: Cyanosulfidic photochemical synthesis of aminoacetonitrile

conditions.catalyst: sulfide minerals and phosphate-buffered photochemical surface system
conditions.other: UV irradiation; cyanosulfidic network analogous to Patel-Sutherland conditions

reactions.mechanism: Integrated UV-driven HCN/H2S/phosphate chemistry generates reduced nitrile products including aminoacetonitrile within a shared systems-chemistry network.
reactions.reasoning: Patel et al. (2015) demonstrated that HCN + H2S + UV + phosphate buffer at 35C generates amino acid precursors including aminoacetonitrile in a unified cyanosulfidic network.

reactions.name: Ferroan brucite mediated synthesis of glycine from glyoxylate and nitrate

conditions.temperature: low-moderate, near ambient to hydrothermal fringe
conditions.other: serpentinite-associated alkaline mineral system; strong mineral partitioning of product

reactions.mechanism: Mineral-mediated reduction/amination couples nitrate-derived nitrogen to glyoxylate on ferroan brucite surfaces, directly forming and sorbing glycine.
reactions.reasoning: The 2024 PNAS study (doi:10.1073/pnas.2408248121) showed ferroan brucite mediates glycine synthesis from nitrate and glyoxylate, with up to 97% of glycine partitioned to the mineral fraction. This is one of the strongest direct hydrothermal routes in the supplied literature.

reactions.name: UV mineral-assisted reversible amination of glyoxylate to glycine

conditions.other: UV illumination; alpha-keto acid amination analog chemistry

reactions.mechanism: Photochemically activated mineral surfaces promote reductive amination/dehydrogenase-like interconversion between glyoxylate and glycine.
reactions.reasoning: Wang et al. (2012) showed ZnS-catalyzed reversible amination of alpha-keto acids under UV, explicitly including glyoxylate to glycine analog chemistry; Kitadai et al. (2017) and Senanayake and Idriss (2006) support UV-active mineral mediation of prebiotic amino-acid chemistry. This step is literature-guided but somewhat broader than the ferroan brucite route, so it should be considered a plausible surface analog rather than the best-established route.

reactions.name: Wet-dry condensation of glycine to glycylglycine

conditions.other: wet-dry cycling on clay/mineral interfaces; peptide bond formation

reactions.mechanism: Concentration and dehydration convert glycine into simple peptides, showing downstream biochemical utility of the synthesized target.
reactions.reasoning: Lahav et al. (1978), Paecht-Horowitz (1976), and Huber and Wachtershauser (1998) support prebiotic peptide formation from amino acids under drying or mineral-assisted conditions; this connects glycine synthesis to biochemical assembly.

reactions.name: Atmospheric/formamide branch from HCN hydrolysis

conditions.other: UV-exposed pond chemistry

reactions.mechanism: Partial hydration/hydrolysis of HCN furnishes formamide as a related nitrogen-carbonyl intermediate in surface photochemistry.
reactions.reasoning: The supplied literature notes HCN + H2O + UV can lead to formamide and broader prebiotic photochemistry, while Senanayake and Idriss (2006) demonstrated TiO2-mediated formamide photochemistry. This branch is included as a connected side reservoir but is not used as a direct glycine-production claim.

pathways.name: Canonical Strecker via methanimine and glycinamide
pathways.description: Formaldehyde and ammonia form aminomethanol, which dehydrates to methanimine. Cyanide addition gives aminoacetonitrile, and sequential hydrolysis through glycinamide yields glycine.

pathways.name: Glycolonitrile-first Strecker branch
pathways.description: Formaldehyde first reacts with HCN to glycolonitrile, then ammonia channels this reservoir into aminoacetonitrile, which hydrolyzes to glycine.

pathways.name: Bucherer-Bergs from aminoacetonitrile
pathways.description: Canonical Strecker chemistry reaches aminoacetonitrile, which is trapped by CO2 as hydantoin and then hydrolyzed through N-carbamoylglycine to glycine.

pathways.name: Bucherer-Bergs from glycolonitrile branch
pathways.description: The glycolonitrile-dominant low-ammonia branch still reaches aminoacetonitrile and then proceeds through hydantoin chemistry to glycine.

pathways.name: Cyanosulfidic aminoacetonitrile to glycine
pathways.description: A unified UV-driven HCN/H2S/phosphate network generates aminoacetonitrile directly, which then hydrolyzes through glycinamide to glycine.

pathways.name: HCN polymer hydrolysis in warm aqueous media
pathways.description: Concentrated HCN oligomerizes to DAMN-rich polymer material that hydrolyzes to amino-acid mixtures with glycine dominant.

pathways.name: Frozen eutectic NH4CN/HCN reservoir to glycine
pathways.description: Under eutectic freezing, HCN concentration crosses the polymerization threshold; the resulting polymer reservoir later hydrolyzes to glycine.

pathways.name: FTT-fed Strecker route
pathways.description: CO-rich reduced gas processing on metallic surfaces supplies formaldehyde and HCN precursor pools that feed canonical Strecker synthesis to glycine.

pathways.name: Direct hydrothermal glyoxylate+nitrate route
pathways.description: Hydrothermal carbon fixation provides glyoxylate, and ferroan brucite couples it with nitrate-derived nitrogen to directly yield glycine while concentrating the product on minerals.

pathways.name: Surface UV amination of hydrothermal glyoxylate
pathways.description: Hydrothermal glyoxylate is transferred to UV-exposed surface settings, where mineral-assisted amination with ammonia yields glycine.
